name: Common cabbage (food medium)
description: Food-derived medium: metabolizable components of Common cabbage (Brassica oleracea) [Vegetables] from FooDB, plus a standard M9 mineral base. Organic components use presence-based uptake bounds; FooDB content retained. Measured compounds with no BiGG exchange are listed under uncovered.

provenance.citation: FooDB v2020-04-07 (Wishart DS et al., foodb.ca). Content sources: DTU, PHENOL EXPLORER, USDA.
provenance.notes: Organic components mapped from FooDB compounds to BiGG via InChIKey/name. Mineral base added. Bounds presence-based; foodb_content retained. Unmapped measured compounds kept in uncovered (v2 completeness fix). IMPORTANT: composition values are taken from the archived FooDB 2020-04-07 CSV data release; FooDB's live food page no longer displays this bulk composition, so it will appear empty. Verify individual compounds via the per-component cross-reference links. Underlying content was aggregated by FooDB from USDA / DTU / Phenol Explorer.
provenance.verification: expert-curated (multi-source food composition; growth-relevant profile completed, FooDB noise removed)
curation_sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 169975 (Cabbage, raw) - full nutrient panel: sugars, amino acids, fatty acids, minerals, vitamins, choline
Johanningsmeier, Fleming & Breidt 2004, J Food Sci 69(8):M222-M227 - raw cabbage malic acid 12.2 +/- 1.6 mM (5-25 mM range), citing Fleming et al. 1988
Aprifel white/green cabbage nutritional sheets - organic acid and macronutrient cross-check
Cabbage GABA survey - green cabbage 3.2-7.1 mg/100 g FW
Oxalate food-composition tables (oxalatecontent.com; Brassica oxalate reviews) - cabbage low-oxalate ~4 mg/100 g
FooDB FOOD00030 (Common cabbage) - baseline profile and phytochemical coverage
provenance.curation_notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources for a microbe growing on cabbage are the free hexoses D-glucose (1.67 g) and D-fructose (1.45 g) plus L-malic acid (~164 mg/100 g, 12.2 mM) - malic acid was entirely absent from the FooDB profile and is the most important addition (it is the substrate for the malolactic reaction central to sauerkraut fermentation). Nitrogen is supplied mainly by glutamate/aspartate-rich protein and a glutamine/glutamate-dominated free amino-acid pool. Fat is negligible (~0.1 g/100
curation_added: L-malic acid (dominant organic acid, 12.2 mM; the single biggest omission)
citric acid, oxalic acid, succinic acid (organic acids, present/quantified where possible)
individual sugars with amounts: D-glucose 1.67, D-fructose 1.45, sucrose 0.08, maltose 0.01 g/100g
L-phenylalanine, L-valine (protein amino acids missing from the FooDB list)
vitamin C, K, B6, niacin, pantothenate, thiamin, riboflavin quantified; vitamin B12 flagged absent
full mineral panel with amounts (K, Ca, P, Na, Mg, Fe, Zn, Mn, Cu, Se)

curation_removed.name: Lycopene
curation_removed.reason: Green/common cabbage contains no lycopene (a tomato/red-fruit carotenoid); FooDB predicted/implausible for this matrix.

curation_removed.name: Retinol
curation_removed.reason: Preformed retinol is animal-only; cabbage supplies vitamin A solely as provitamin-A carotenoids (alpha/beta-carotene). Implausible as a plant nutrient.

curation_removed.name: Starch C12H20O10
curation_removed.reason: USDA reports 0 g starch in raw cabbage; not a real quantitative carbon source (kept as 0/trace note, not a growth substrate).

curation_removed.name: Co2+
curation_removed.reason: Free cobalt(II) ion is not a defined cabbage nutrient at meaningful levels; trace contaminant-level, no growth relevance.

curation_removed.name: Nickel
curation_removed.reason: Trace element at non-nutritive levels; not a defined growth nutrient of cabbage.

curation_removed.name: Iodine c
curation_removed.reason: Only trace/variable levels (soil-dependent), unquantified; not a defining cabbage nutrient.

components.name: L-malic acid
components.evidence_source: Johanningsmeier, Fleming & Breidt 2004 J Food Sci 69(8):M222-M227 citing Fleming et al. 1988

components.name: citric acid
components.evidence_source: Aprifel white cabbage sheet; multiple HPLC organic-acid studies of Brassica oleracea

components.name: succinic acid
components.evidence_source: Sauerkraut/cabbage fermentation HPLC panels (present in fresh cabbage baseline)

components.name: quercetin, kaempferol, myricetin, isorhamnetin, apigenin, luteolin (genuine cabbage flavonoids)

uncovered.name: total sugars
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: dietary fiber
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: gamma-aminobutyric acid
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: palmitic acid
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: cyanidin, delphinidin, peonidin, pelargonidin (anthocyanidins - genuine, mainly in red/purple cabbage, low in green)
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

uncovered.name: sinigrin, glucobrassicin and other glucosinolates (genuine defining Brassica phytochemicals; no BiGG met)
uncovered.note: no exchange auto-assigned; propose uptake -1.0 pending manual curation

oxygen_note: food substrate; O2 availability is a simulation condition, not a property of the food
basis: per 100 g fresh weight (raw green/white head cabbage, Brassica oleracea var. capitata)
